result:
The first thing I learned in the first apartment that ever felt like mine was that a bicycle chain will tell you about entropy better than any physics textbook. There it was, every morning by the door, a little line of metal loops that somehow bore the memory of every rainy alley I’d ridden through and every day I’d postponed the quiet work of care. When it was clean and oiled, the ride hummed past shop windows and trees with that pleasing sense of having found a groove in the world. When it wasn’t, the whole day carried the tat-tat of resistance in my ears, a reminder that everything on this earth is in a long conversation with rust, and the only question is how attentive you’ll be to the back-and-forth.

We don’t get much ceremony for those battles against the creeping fray. We talk about breakthroughs and revolutions, about disruptions and launches and dawns of eras, but the bulk of any life—person or city or planet—happens in maintenance. Even the word feels like cement dust. And yet, when I think of the times I have felt safest, or necessary, or in love, they cluster around acts of humble upkeep. A patch on a favorite sweater done with bright thread. A friend who remembered a complicated coffee order after months. A library shelving shift at closing time, the aisles quiet, hands smoothing spines into alphabetic order like a baker tucking dough into pans.

A city, seen this way, is a series of circles rather than lines. The garbage truck comes Monday and Thursday at 7 a.m. The little park is mown into an even stubble that absorbs footsteps with that surprising sponginess of well-watered grass. The sewer grates chuff and suck after storms, clearing throats you didn’t even think to notice. Somewhere, a water treatment plant thrums, and tanks are backwashed, and chemicals are measured out so precisely that when we turn a handle, what flows is transparent and tasteless and life-giving rather than an alchemical industrial soup. It’s easy to imagine that someone else is in charge of this orchestra, some giant whose job it is to keep the lights on. But it’s never a single someone. It’s a thousand someones, all of them with calendars and calloused hands, most of them living, at least professionally, below the horizon of attention.

I once toured a municipal water facility because I happen to be friends with a person who loves valves. It wasn’t a glamorous invitation, but I said yes, because I am also a person who loves valves. The place had the smell of wet stone and faint bleach, and no windows at all except narrow panes up high where a colleague had taped a paper cutout of a sun with a smiling face: Hello, Day Shift! Below were labyrinths of pipes that looked like diagrams of arteries, and there's a kind of reverence you feel, looking at a machine so committed to life. There were charts on clipboards, some digital screens (even water plants have their dashboards), and a wall of binders labeled with dates and systems in a precise black hand. This binder, one technician said, is the most important thing we have. It was the maintenance log. If something went off, it told them not only what might be wrong but the entire path by which they had gotten to this exact present. The present is always the crescendo of previous touches.

I use the word “touches” deliberately, because maintenance is sensory and intimate even when it takes place at scale. It’s ears that know the difference between a healthy bearing and one starting to complain in a voice only a trained listener would recognize. It’s fingers that can slip a single thread through the tension tensioners of a sewing machine with a kind of muscle memory that almost looks like magic. It’s a nose that knows something is burning a little, not because smoke is visible yet, but because the air has that faint acridness that is the body language of varnished wood under stress.

Think about sourdough starter in a jar. People talk about it as if it were a pet, and maybe that’s not entirely a joke. You feed it when it’s hungry, you learn its moods. It fusses when you have neglected it. You don’t get to just “build” bread one time and call it finished; you inhabit a rhythm with it. The baker wakes early, folds and rests and shapes and waits, all so that when the loaves cool on wire racks and people walk home holding them with the same small smile reserved for bouquets, the bread is as close as you can get to the ideal loaf in this particular kitchen with this particular air and this particular person doing the work.

There’s a quiet but profound ethics to this. Building is an assertion: I want this to exist. Maintenance is a different kind of vow: I will keep this alive. The second vow is harder to glamorize because it courts tedium and doesn’t resolve. You don’t “finish” sweeping the floor. You have to sweep it again next week. But that also makes it a stance against the fantasy that life is a series of irreversible moments. The gift of maintenance is that it understands the self not as a brand-new device but as a living system. Your teeth will require flossing and food, your friendships will require calls and jokes and forgiveness, your codebase will require patches, your garden will require mulching and the acceptance that weeds are not villains but opportunists you must outlast.

The older I get, the more that last point matters. I had a friend in her seventies who kept a garden that—if you stood back—looked like a dream of an orderly meadow. She would tell you, if you asked, that it was neither orderly nor a meadow. It was a conversation with soil. “I start with compost,” she said, “because it’s proof that decay is only one direction you can steer the energy of a thing. Also, it smells like a forest after rain.” She used the word steer and not stop. No one stops decay. Tending is always steering, small course corrections, patient nudges. She would kneel and coax a volunteer tomato plant along a trellis that had not been built for it but could be convinced to bear its weight. She would pull nettles and not apologize to them, then take them inside to make tea, both to ironize the plant’s sting and because it made her feel resourceful. When she died, the garden did what gardens do without primary authors: it changed into something else. The idea of leaving anything in such a state—that it could keep living, altered but nourished—is to me the highest achievement of someone who loves maintenance.

We live, though, in a time when the story is more often about the launch or the pivot, and the abandonment of the old version in favor of the new. There’s an economic logic for that. Maintenance is cost without the fireworks of novelty. A highway’s ribbon-cutting is a photo op. The repaving budget is a line item that provokes snarky letters to the editor. But we’re surrounded now by the evidence that deferred maintenance is not even a neutral option; it’s a debt with interest. Bridges crack. Dikes fail. Subways stall. Buildings sweat in heat waves not accounted for in their designs. Devices that require proprietary screws become unrepairable even for the people who want to do the right thing. Underneath it all is a human feeling: losing the tie between attentive care and durable belonging.

I once had a neighbor who was a watch repairer in a shop that had outlasted many other businesses on our block. He was not a romantic. He swore at springs and loupes and occasionally at people. But he took a fierce, almost parental pride in watches that came in as little tragedies—“He jumped into a lake with it on,” “It fell from the dresser”—and left with their fine ticks restored. The work was small. He wore finger cots and breathed like a swimmer, sparing his watchful eye the fog. “People come in here with their grandfather,” he said once, meaning the device not the man. “It’s three generations of birthdays in a case. It deserves a second chance at the fourth.” He liked that his work was invisible. “If you can see it,” he said, “I have failed.” The great paradox of such craft is that its reward lies in erasing all sign of struggle. The world is full of this invisible labor: the carefully invisible seam on a hem, the invisible fix to a database, the invisible decision not to say the snippy thing in a conversation because you are maintaining a relationship rather than winning an exchange.

Software folks often joke that there’s no such thing as finished software; there is only software that has been abandoned. They are not wrong. What is interesting is the way the best of them manage the ongoingness without succumbing to despair. There are some practices: tests that run like little bedtime rituals, code reviews that act like peer care rather than hazing. Documentation that treats the future reader as a collaborator you already like. People use the word “sustainable” so often that it turns into paste, but it’s a good word in this context because it points not only to resource restraint but to a right-sized ambition for lives that must be lived within limits. It’s utopian to imagine a product that will work perfectly and forever; it’s mature to build systems in which the imperfect and the forever-changing can be supported without constant emergency.

My favorite phrase in management—again, not an obviously romantic discipline—is “bus factor.” It is gruesome and pragmatic at once: how many people on your team would have to be hit by a bus for the project to be in mortal danger? It’s a measure of hoarded knowledge, of undocumented logic, of the personality cults that often develop around the cleverest engineer or the smoothest organizer. Good maintenance cultures lower the bus factor by distributing understanding. They do this not only by writing things down, which is often touted as the solution, but by embedding redundancy in the ecology of relationships itself. Even better, they do it by fostering an ethic that cares more for the long-term resilience of the work than for the short-term glory of the solver.

When we talk about relationships in the context of maintenance, people feel itchy, because relationships marketed as chores quickly become joyless. But that’s not the right metaphor either. The recurring work in any relationship—making tea, putting down the phone when someone is talking, saying “tell me more”—isn’t like sweeping a floor you resent. It is like tending a small fire you want to keep through the night. There is a rhythm to that tending that, when you get it even half-right, actually produces more warmth than any one big dramatic act. The grand gesture is wonderful. Bring the flowers. Book the trip. Write the note. But there is a steadier art in noticing that, when she comes home late, she always wants to stand in the doorway five minutes before she takes off her coat, and in letting that be the opening through which the day enters your shared space. There is maintenance in the apology offered within an hour rather than a week. There is maintenance in learning to cut the scallions the way your grandmother did because it tastes like childhood to someone you love.

It would be easy to stop here and say, See, we just need to value maintenance more, and then make a list of things that need tending and be on our way. But I think the harder truth is that the speed and novelty addiction of our culture has grown into us, so it’s not just institutions that need recalibration; it’s our own nervous systems. Deep in the body of anyone who lives online or near cities or within ambitious families is a feeling that the new thing is a kind of rescue. That’s sometimes true. New tools and new forms can liberate. But they can also make the old work harder to do even when the old work is the work that allows the new to matter. Try explaining to a sixteen-year-old what the DMCA is and why their printer’s ink cartridges won’t refill even though ink itself has no special metaphysical property that should prevent it from flowing again. Try telling yourself that buying the new phone will not, in fact, solve the bad feeling of being behind on messages.

There’s a paradox that haunts the modern appetite. We want frictionless experience so that we can “save time,” but the time we save doesn’t, in aggregate, go toward deeper, slower, richer care. It gets filled with more commitments that then require their own maintenance. The great mercy in this muddle is that the body is stubborn. It gets tired. It says no even when you’ve promised it will say yes. You will skip a night shift sometimes. You will break the chain. The bicycle will grind. You will be reminded that rest is not a passive absence of activity but a maintenance practice in its own right. That’s the one we’re worst at, as a culture: the maintenance of the human vessel. Sleep has no launch party. But sleep gets more done than ambition, when you look at a whole month rather than a single “crushing it” day.

It’s possible, in all this, to sound like a crank aligned with the church of routine. I do love routines. I also love the shock of the new. The secret is not to choose one and banish the other but to get good at interleaving them. My favorite example is the lighthouse. A lighthouse is maintenance in the form of a miracle. It flashes the same light, over and over, to say, Here is the edge. Don’t smash yourself. Within that repetition, ships cross oceans, storms roll, and maps are redrawn. The light does not need to be innovative to be essential. It needs to be there, in hunger and boredom and resolve, because the cost of its absence is too high. There is perhaps no better image of care that can endure the drama of change without being swallowed by it.

The emerging wave of machines that talk, reason, suggest, and summon data feels like a shift as big as any town becoming a city. People ask whether these systems will replace us, or make us lazy, or alienate us from the tactile world even more than we already are. Those are good questions. There is another I think about: if the machines can compose a convincing email or digest a report or answer a question, what would we let it free us for? The cynical answer is: more tasks to cram into the day. The gentler possibility is that assistance with drudgery could be an ally to maintenance—not as a replacement for the human touch, but as a widening of our attention for the kinds of care only we can do.

Can a system set a reminder that a plant needs watering? Yes. Can it water the plant? Sometimes. Can it notice that a friend’s eyes are a little dull and decide to make soup? Not yet, and I am not sure I want it to. The skill of presence is the skill under all other skills; the operand of affection is noticing. Machines can scaffold, remind, nudge, and catch our mistakes; they can store the logbook, suggest that the filter is due, predict the failure before it comes. That’s plenty. If we give them that role with humility, we might get back a few handfuls of human hours for the kind of maintenance that looks like sitting with someone who is grieving, or mending the knee of a child’s jeans, or showing up to the neighborhood meeting where someone will dutifully present the sewer upgrade plan and someone else will be predictably grumpy and the whole thing will feel like a rehearsal for the ancient play called We Keep This Going.

At the city scale, this play is becoming climate theater. The weather we trained for is not the weather we have. That means the maintenance practices that used to be “good enough” are now how we lose a town in a flood or keep it. There are communities where volunteers sandbag in the moonlight when the river speaks louder than usual. There are also quieter, peacetime practices: clearing culverts of the debris that would otherwise magnify a storm into a catastrophe. Updating building codes even though that makes some projects drag on. Planting trees in places where the shade will shave dangerous degrees off the sidewalk temperature fifteen years from now. The verb in all this is adapt, which is only the complicated cousin of maintain. Your effort is not against change. Your effort is for continuity through change.

I think of archives. Not the glamorous ones full of letters by famous people, but the municipal archives that hold minutes of meetings where a town debated whether to pave a road or how much it should cost to license a dog. There is so much life in those lines, and so much care poured into words that, at the time, must have felt slightly ridiculous: read aloud, debated, voted on, recorded. The archivists, most of whom will never be thanked for their work, build a memory palace for everyone else to search in crisis. When the question arises—has this happened before?—the archive is how you know. If the answer is yes, you can learn. If the answer is no, you can be honest about that too. In a maintenance culture, truth is an ally because it gives you the chance to adjust in time rather than in the wake of disaster. Denial is the enemy of maintenance.

Maintenance has enemies, and not only neglect. There’s also purity. The insistence that any deviation from the ideal is failure. The home baker who throws away a loaf because the crust is not the exact shade of auburn they saw on a glossy feed; the boss who rejects a straightforward fix because it offends the idea of elegance; the new homeowner who rips out a healthy but messy hedge because it doesn’t look like the magazine. Perfectionism is entropic in its own way: it stops the flow of care because it turns the field into a landmine. Better to celebrate the wonky and the working than to break the spirit of the person who tried and might try again tomorrow.

This is why I like visible mending, the movement that repairs clothes with stitches that are not only obvious but joyful. Bright thread across the pale knee of denim, patterned patches that announce themselves. They don’t only save the garment. They tell a story of repair that can travel from stranger to stranger on a subway, a soft billboard for the idea that making-do is not shameful but a form of dignity. That spirit scales into the repair cafés that have been springing up, where people haul in lamps that don’t light and radios that don’t sing and get help to coax them back to usefulness. Knowledge is passed hand to hand; someone learns to use a soldering iron; someone else brings muffins. The world is full of rooms like this if you look. They are not often in the headlines. They are where hope grows its roots.

Out beyond the rooms people knit and solder in, there are also long continuities of maintenance we don’t often think about because their time scales dwarf ours. A forest maintains itself through cycles of fire and regrowth, through the extraordinary collaboration of fungi and tree roots we call the wood-wide web, trading sugars and messages like an ancient market. Coral reefs maintain, or used to, their delicate balancings of predators and grazers. A volcano maintains a pressure that occasionally overflows. All this existing tunedness is not an invitation to leave it alone always—we’ve intervened too much for that fantasy—but it is a reminder that maintenance is not always handiwork. It can also look like reducing the harm we add to the systems that are already doing their balancing acts, and offering support when they are overwhelmed by pressures we introduced.

Back in the kitchen, there’s a pot of beans simmering. Not because beans are sexy, but because a pot of beans repaired a string of Tuesday nights when I didn’t have the bandwidth for invention. They are forgiveness in edible form. So is the freezer, with its stash of soup for the days when no one can cook. So is the email draft folder with the unsent thank you note that will, when finally sent, repair a small neglect. These are homely examples. They are what life is made of.

People sometimes ask, in a way that is both touching and exasperated, what they should do with their lives. I am not qualified to answer on the grand level. But I have one small suggestion that helps at any scale: find something worth maintaining, and commit to the quiet excellence of its care. It might be your mother tongue. It might be a block where you live. It might be a tradition that seems fated to be forgotten unless someone—maybe you—carries it forward, not as a museum piece but as a living practice. It might be the habit of reading aloud to children. It might be a stream. It might be your own patience.

And then, because this is not all sermons, make it fun where you can. Play music while you oil the chain. Keep a silly logbook if you must, with doodles of the machines you’ve tamed for the day. Invite a friend to join you in the cleaning and make a ritual of pizza afterward. Humans are not maintenance machines. We’re animals that love meaning. If the meaning has to be partly invented, so be it. Invention in the service of care is a holy kind of lying.

Someday I will move from this apartment where the bicycle leans, and someone else will live with these floors and these familiar scuffs. They will find, perhaps, a loose tile in the kitchen that I never got around to cementing properly. They will curse me for it, briefly, then fix it, then forget. There is humility and consolation in that imagined moment. Our maintenance is never forever. It is for the day, the year, if we’re lucky, the generation. You inherit the world in a condition not of your choosing. You pass it along, if you have chosen well, in a condition someone else can keep choosing. This is love, shorn of grandeur and sure of purpose.

The chain squeaks a little today. I notice. I find the oil, wipe the grit, turn the pedals, watch the glistening. It takes three minutes. The rest of the ride is not a revelation. It’s just smooth. Which, in a world whose default is friction, might count as a daily miracle. I get where I’m going feeling like I didn’t have to argue with the morning to earn my arrival. Later, perhaps, I will finally sew the button back on that coat. Or text that old friend. Or cook enough beans to fill a neighbor’s container too. These are tiny things. Maybe you could list a thousand of them. That’s what you might call a life. Not built once, then applauded. Kept, in its messy way, tended. Kept again tomorrow. A work of maintenance that, in its persistence, is indistinguishable from devotion.

It says no even when you’ve promised it will say yes. You will skip a night shift sometimes. You will break the chain. The bicycle will grind. You will be reminded that rest is not a passive absence of activity but a maintenance practice in its own right. That’s the one we’re worst at, as a culture: the maintenance of the human vessel. Sleep has no launch party. But sleep gets more done than ambition, when you look at a whole month rather than a single “crushing it” day.

It’s possible, in all this, to sound like a crank aligned with the church of routine. I do love routines. I also love the shock of the new. The secret is not to choose one and banish the other but to get good at interleaving them. My favorite example is the lighthouse. A lighthouse is maintenance in the form of a miracle. It flashes the same light, over and over, to say, Here is the edge. Don’t smash yourself. Within that repetition, ships cross oceans, storms roll, and maps are redrawn. The light does not need to be innovative to be essential. It needs to be there, in hunger and boredom and resolve, because the cost of its absence is too high. There is perhaps no better image of care that can endure the drama of change without being swallowed by it.

The emerging wave of machines that talk, reason, suggest, and summon data feels like a shift as big as any town becoming a city. People ask whether these systems will replace us, or make us lazy, or alienate us from the tactile world even more than we already are. Those are good questions. There is another I think about: if the machines can compose a convincing email or digest a report or answer a question, what would we let it free us for? The cynical answer is: more tasks to cram into the day. The gentler possibility is that assistance with drudgery could be an ally to maintenance—not as a replacement for the human touch, but as a widening of our attention for the kinds of care only we can do.

Can a system set a reminder that a plant needs watering? Yes. Can it water the plant? Sometimes. Can it notice that a friend’s eyes are a little dull and decide to make soup? Not yet, and I am not sure I want it to. The skill of presence is the skill under all other skills; the operand of affection is noticing. Machines can scaffold, remind, nudge, and catch our mistakes; they can store the logbook, suggest that the filter is due, predict the failure before it comes. That’s plenty. If we give them that role with humility, we might get back a few handfuls of human hours for the kind of maintenance that looks like sitting with someone who is grieving, or mending the knee of a child’s jeans, or showing up to the neighborhood meeting where someone will dutifully present the sewer upgrade plan and someone else will be predictably grumpy and the whole thing will feel like a rehearsal for the ancient play called We Keep This Going.

At the city scale, this play is becoming climate theater. The weather we trained for is not the weather we have. That means the maintenance practices that used to be “good enough” are now how we lose a town in a flood or keep it. There are communities where volunteers sandbag in the moonlight when the river speaks louder than usual. There are also quieter, peacetime practices: clearing culverts of the debris that would otherwise magnify a storm into a catastrophe. Updating building codes even though that makes some projects drag on. Planting trees in places where the shade will shave dangerous degrees off the sidewalk temperature fifteen years from now. The verb in all this is adapt, which is only the complicated cousin of maintain. Your effort is not against change. Your effort is for continuity through change.

I think of archives. Not the glamorous ones full of letters by famous people, but the municipal archives that hold minutes of meetings where a town debated whether to pave a road or how much it should cost to license a dog. There is so much life in those lines, and so much care poured into words that, at the time, must have felt slightly ridiculous: read aloud, debated, voted on, recorded. The archivists, most of whom will never be thanked for their work, build a memory palace for everyone else to search in crisis. When the question arises—has this happened before?—the archive is how you know. If the answer is yes, you can learn. If the answer is no, you can be honest about that too. In a maintenance culture, truth is an ally because it gives you the chance to adjust in time rather than in the wake of disaster. Denial is the enemy of maintenance.

Maintenance has enemies, and not only neglect. There’s also purity. The insistence that any deviation from the ideal is failure. The home baker who throws away a loaf because the crust is not the exact shade of auburn they saw on a glossy feed; the boss who rejects a straightforward fix because it offends the idea of elegance; the new homeowner who rips out a healthy but messy hedge because it doesn’t look like the magazine. Perfectionism is entropic in its own way: it stops the flow of care because it turns the field into a landmine. Better to celebrate the wonky and the working than to break the spirit of the person who tried and might try again tomorrow.

This is why I like visible mending, the movement that repairs clothes with stitches that are not only obvious but joyful. Bright thread across the pale knee of denim, patterned patches that announce themselves. They don’t only save the garment. They tell a story of repair that can travel from stranger to stranger on a subway, a soft billboard for the idea that making-do is not shameful but a form of dignity. That spirit scales into the repair cafés that have been springing up, where people haul in lamps that don’t light and radios that don’t sing and get help to coax them back to usefulness. Knowledge is passed hand to hand; someone learns to use a soldering iron; someone else brings muffins. The world is full of rooms like this if you look. They are not often in the headlines. They are where hope grows its roots.

Out beyond the rooms people knit and solder in, there are also long continuities of maintenance we don’t often think about because their time scales dwarf ours. A forest maintains itself through cycles of fire and regrowth, through the extraordinary collaboration of fungi and tree roots we call the wood-wide web, trading sugars and messages like an ancient market. Coral reefs maintain, or used to, their delicate balancings of predators and grazers. A volcano maintains a pressure that occasionally overflows. All this existing tunedness is not an invitation to leave it alone always—we’ve intervened too much for that fantasy—but it is a reminder that maintenance is not always handiwork. It can also look like reducing the harm we add to the systems that are already doing their balancing acts, and offering support when they are overwhelmed by pressures we introduced.

Back in the kitchen, there’s a pot of beans simmering. Not because beans are sexy, but because a pot of beans repaired a string of Tuesday nights when I didn’t have the bandwidth for invention. They are forgiveness in edible form. So is the freezer, with its stash of soup for the days when no one can cook. So is the email draft folder with the unsent thank you note that will, when finally sent, repair a small neglect. These are homely examples. They are what life is made of.

People sometimes ask, in a way that is both touching and exasperated, what they should do with their lives. I am not qualified to answer on the grand level. But I have one small suggestion that helps at any scale: find something worth maintaining, and commit to the quiet excellence of its care. It might be your mother tongue. It might be a block where you live. It might be a tradition that seems fated to be forgotten unless someone—maybe you—carries it forward, not as a museum piece but as a living practice. It might be the habit of reading aloud to children. It might be a stream. It might be your own patience.

And then, because this is not all sermons, make it fun where you can. Play music while you oil the chain. Keep a silly logbook if you must, with doodles of the machines you’ve tamed for the day. Invite a friend to join you in the cleaning and make a ritual of pizza afterward. Humans are not maintenance machines. We’re animals that love meaning. If the meaning has to be partly invented, so be it. Invention in the service of care is a holy kind of lying.

Someday I will move from this apartment where the bicycle leans, and someone else will live with these floors and these familiar scuffs. They will find, perhaps, a loose tile in the kitchen that I never got around to cementing properly. They will curse me for it, briefly, then fix it, then forget. There is humility and consolation in that imagined moment. Our maintenance is never forever. It is for the day, the year, if we’re lucky, the generation. You inherit the world in a condition not of your choosing. You pass it along, if you have chosen well, in a condition someone else can keep choosing. This is love, shorn of grandeur and sure of purpose.

The chain squeaks a little today. I notice. I find the oil, wipe the grit, turn the pedals, watch the glistening. It takes three minutes. The rest of the ride is not a revelation. It’s just smooth. Which, in a world whose default is friction, might count as a daily miracle. I get where I’m going feeling like I didn’t have to argue with the morning to earn my arrival. Later, perhaps, I will finally sew the button back on that coat. Or text that old friend. Or cook enough beans to fill a neighbor’s container too. These are tiny things. Maybe you could list a thousand of them. That’s what you might call a life. Not built once, then applauded. Kept, in its messy way, tended. Kept again tomorrow. A work of maintenance that, in its persistence, is indistinguishable from devotion.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
